title: Confidence: The Cartoon That Helped America Get Through the Great Depression (1933)
description:

ActuôÙalôÙly, hold up a sec. Weãll
body:
in Animation, History, Music |   	May 6th, 2026  Leave a Comment
	
			

	

No more bumôÙminã, letãs all get to workãÎ
ActuôÙalôÙly, hold up a sec. Weãll all be hapôÙpiôÙer and more proôÙducôÙtive if we take a moment to start our work day with ConôÙfiôÙdence, a pepôÙpy musiôÙcal aniôÙmaôÙtion from 1933, starôÙring newôÙly electôÙed PresôÙiôÙdentô Franklin Delano RooôÙsevelt and MickôÙey Mouse preôÙcurôÙsor,ô Oswald the Lucky RabôÙbit.ô 
Few Americansãtoday weãd refer to them as the 1%ãcould escape the priôÙvaôÙtions of the Great DepresôÙsion.ô The movies were one indusôÙtry that conôÙtinôÙued to thrive through this dark periôÙod, preôÙciseôÙly because they offered a few hours of respite. No one went to the picôÙtures to see a reflecôÙtion of their own lives. GorôÙgeous gowns, glamôÙorous ManôÙhatôÙtan apartôÙments and romanôÙtic trouôÙble cerôÙtainô to be resolved in hapôÙpy endingsãÎrememberô Mia FarôÙrowãs beleaôÙgueredô waitôÙressô baskôÙing inô the PurôÙple Rose of Cairoãsô reasôÙsurôÙing glow?
GivôÙen the pubôÙlicãs prefôÙerôÙence for escapist fare, direcôÙtor Bill Nolan, the Father of RubôÙber Hose AniôÙmaôÙtion, could have played it safe by glossôÙing over the backôÙstoôÙry that leads Oswald to seek out advice fromô the ComôÙmanôÙder in Chief. Instead, Nolan delivôÙered his joyôÙful carôÙtoon aniôÙmals into nightôÙmare terôÙriôÙtoôÙry, the DepresôÙsion perôÙsonôÙiôÙfied as a cowled Death figôÙure layôÙing waste to the land. Itãs weirdôÙly upsetôÙting to see those hyper-cheerôÙful vinôÙtage barnôÙyard aniôÙmals (and a rogue monôÙkey) underôÙgo this graphôÙicô enerôÙvaôÙtion.
Oh, for some oral historyãIãd love to know how matiôÙnee crowds reactôÙed as Oswald raced screamôÙing before a spinôÙning verôÙtiôÙgo backôÙground, seekôÙing a remôÙeôÙdy for a host of non-carôÙtoon probôÙlems. Irony is a luxôÙuôÙry they didôÙnãt have.
UnsurôÙprisôÙingôÙly,ô the can-do spirôÙit so cenôÙtral to FDRãs New Deal quickôÙly turned Oswaldãs frown upside down. As presôÙiôÙdenôÙtial camôÙpaign promisôÙes go, this oneãs uniqueôÙlyô taiôÙlored to the demands ofô musiôÙcal comôÙeôÙdy. WitôÙness Annie, in which the 32nd presôÙiôÙdent wasô again called uponô to Rex HarôÙriôÙson his way into audiôÙence hearts, this time fromô the wheelôÙchair the creôÙators of ConôÙfiôÙdence didôÙnãt dare show, some forty years earôÙliôÙer.
The diviôÙsion between enterôÙtainôÙment and nation-leadôÙing is pretôÙty perôÙmeôÙable these days, too.
AccordôÙingôÙly, what realôÙly sets this carôÙtoon apart for me is the use of a PresôÙiôÙdenôÙtialôÙly-sancôÙtioned giant syringe as a tool to get DepresôÙsion-era AmerôÙiôÙca back on its feet. A figôÙuôÙraôÙtive injecôÙtion of conôÙfiôÙdence is all well and good, but nothôÙing gets the barnôÙyardô back on its singing, dancôÙing feet like a libôÙerôÙal dose, delivôÙered in the most litôÙerôÙal way.
Note: An earôÙliôÙer verôÙsion of this post appeared on our site in 2014.
RelatôÙed ConôÙtent:
A SimôÙple, Down-to-Earth ChristôÙmas Card from the Great DepresôÙsion (1933)
PriôÙvate SnaôÙfu: The World War II ProôÙpaôÙganôÙda CarôÙtoons CreôÙatôÙed by Dr. Seuss, Frank Capra & Mel Blanc
Yale Presents an Archive of 170,000 PhoôÙtographs DocôÙuôÙmentôÙing the Great DepresôÙsion
Great DepresôÙsion CookôÙing: Get BudôÙget-MindôÙed Meals from the Online CookôÙing Show CreôÙatôÙed by 93-Year-Old Clara CanôÙnucôÙciaôÙri
When Al Capone Opened a Soup Kitchen DurôÙing the Great DepresôÙsion: AnothôÙer Side of the LegôÙendary Mobsterãs OperôÙaôÙtion
Ayun HalôÙlôÙiôÙday canãt get enough of that rubôÙber style.ô

title: 1,000 Years of Medieval European History in 20 Minutes
description:
in History |   	May 6th, 2026  Leave a Comment
	
			

	

More than a few medievalôÙists object to the term ãDark Agesã as applied to the periôÙod in w
body:
in History |   	May 6th, 2026  Leave a Comment
	
			

	

More than a few medievalôÙists object to the term ãDark Agesã as applied to the periôÙod in which they speôÙcialôÙize. That can seem wishôÙful in light of most comôÙparôÙisons between medieval times and the RenaisôÙsance that came afterôÙward, or indeed, the era of the Roman Empire that came before. ConôÙsidôÙer the state of Europe as the fourth cenôÙtuôÙry began: ãThe great cities of antiqôÙuiôÙty were depopôÙuôÙlatôÙed, some left in ruins,ã says the narôÙraôÙtor of the How So video above, telling the stoôÙry of the conôÙtiôÙnenôÙtãs politôÙiôÙcal and linôÙguisôÙtic fragôÙmenôÙtaôÙtion. ãThe Roman transôÙportaôÙtion sysôÙtem decayed, erodôÙing comôÙmuôÙniôÙcaôÙtion and long-disôÙtance trade. Coins vanôÙished, leavôÙing no ecoôÙnomôÙic sysôÙtem to supôÙport proôÙfesôÙsionôÙal armies. LitôÙerôÙaôÙcy plumôÙmetôÙed, cripôÙpling adminôÙisôÙtraôÙtive sysôÙtems. And most notably, peace and secuôÙriôÙty were gone.ã
But thereãs plenôÙty more hisôÙtoôÙry to come thereôÙafter: about a milôÙlenôÙniôÙumãs worth, in fact, which the video covôÙers in a mere twenôÙty minôÙutes. Events of note in that grand sweep include JusôÙtinôÙian Iãs attempt to expand the ByzanôÙtine Empire of the east; the creôÙation and spread of the IslamôÙic caliphate; CharleôÙmagôÙneãs uniôÙfiôÙcaôÙtion of most of westôÙern ChrisôÙtenôÙdom; invaôÙsions by Vikings, MagôÙyars, and MusôÙlim raiders; the rise of casôÙtles and the feuôÙdal sysôÙtem that they came to symôÙbolôÙize; the creôÙation of the Holy Roman Empire; the flourôÙishôÙing of cities and uniôÙverôÙsiôÙties; and the NorôÙman ConôÙquest of EngôÙland, as seen on the Bayeux TapesôÙtry. Thereãs also the unpleasôÙantôÙness of the Black Death, which swept through Europe from the mid-fourôÙteenth to the earôÙly sixôÙteenth cenôÙtuôÙry ã butô as with othôÙer medieval disôÙasôÙters, the plague held the seeds of a civôÙiôÙlizaôÙtionôÙal rebirth.
ãFor some surôÙvivors, the conôÙseôÙquences of the plague were not so grim,ã says the narôÙraôÙtor. ãAs the popôÙuôÙlaôÙtion dropped, land became wideôÙly availôÙable, and the demand for labor rose draôÙmatôÙiôÙcalôÙly.ã PeasôÙants demandôÙed improved conôÙdiôÙtions and revoltôÙed against the rulers who refused; ultiôÙmateôÙly, they ãgained new freeôÙdoms and opporôÙtuôÙniôÙties, and workôÙers enjoyed highôÙer wages. CreôÙativôÙiôÙty and innoôÙvaôÙtion in sciôÙence and culôÙture folôÙlowed, creôÙatôÙing the enviôÙronôÙment in which EuroôÙpean scholôÙars ãdefined the past milôÙlenôÙniôÙum as ãDark Ages,ã and so posiôÙtioned themôÙselves as the tranôÙsiôÙtion between the medieval and modôÙern world.ã Some liken the curôÙrent state of the world to the decline of the Roman Empire; if theyãre corôÙrect, maybe we have anothôÙer RenaisôÙsance to look forôÙward to about 40 genôÙerôÙaôÙtions down the road.
RelatôÙed ConôÙtent:

How EveryôÙthing in a Medieval CasôÙtle Worked, from Its Moats to Its DunôÙgeons
What Sex Was Like in Medieval Times?: HisôÙtoôÙriôÙans Look at How PeoôÙple Got It On in the Dark Ages
How the ByzanôÙtine Empire Rose, Fell, and CreôÙatôÙed the GloôÙriôÙous Hagia Sophia: A HisôÙtoôÙry in Ten AniôÙmatôÙed MinôÙutes
Advice for Time TravôÙelôÙing to Medieval Europe: How to Stay Healthy & Safe, and AvoidôÙing Charges of WitchôÙcraft
Based in Seoul,ô ColôÙinô Marshallô writes and broadôÙcasts on cities, lanôÙguage, and culôÙture. Heãs the author of the newsletôÙterô Books on Citiesô as well as the booksô ÚõçÙ šš§ õ¡šÏô (No SumôÙmaôÙrizôÙing Korea) andô KoreôÙan Newtro.ô FolôÙlow him on the social netôÙwork forôÙmerôÙly known as TwitôÙter atô @colinmarshall.

title: Why Ancient Egyptian Honey Remains Edible After 3,000 Years
description:
in Film, Food & Drink, History |   	May 5th, 2026  1 Comment
	
			
The globôÙal bee popôÙuôÙlaôÙtion comes up in the news every now and again. SomeôÙtime
body:
in Film, Food & Drink, History |   	May 5th, 2026  1 Comment
	
			
The globôÙal bee popôÙuôÙlaôÙtion comes up in the news every now and again. SomeôÙtimes weãre assured that the numôÙber is staôÙble or risôÙing; more often, weãre warned about colôÙlapsôÙing colonies and the large-scale ecoôÙlogôÙiôÙcal disôÙasôÙter that could result. As with most high-stakes issues, it can be difôÙfiôÙcult to know what to believe. But even if you lack the time to invest in an underôÙstandôÙing of the sciôÙence behind the comôÙplex conôÙnecôÙtions between apiôÙan and human welôÙfare, you can easôÙiôÙly come to appreôÙciôÙate the imporôÙtance of bees if you learn just how long theyãve played a role in our civôÙiôÙlizaôÙtion.
As Elana SpiôÙvack writes at History.com, ãa cave paintôÙing in northôÙeastôÙern Spain depictôÙing a human harôÙvestôÙing honôÙey dates back 7,500 years to the NeolithôÙic periôÙod, accordôÙing to research pubôÙlished in 2021 in the jourôÙnal TraôÙbaôÙjos de PreôÙhisôÙtoôÙria.ã Just last year, a paper in the JourôÙnal of the AmerôÙiôÙcan ChemôÙiôÙcal SociôÙetyô conôÙfirmed that bronze conôÙtainôÙers disôÙcovôÙered in an underôÙground shrine in a sixth-cenôÙtuôÙry-BC Greek setôÙtleôÙment not far from PomôÙpeii conôÙtained a residue of honôÙey. Weãve long known of hieroôÙglyphs from ancient Egypt that depict bees and the keepôÙing thereôÙof; ãaccordôÙing to a 2022 paper in the jourôÙnal AniôÙmals, the use of honôÙeyôÙbees in the Nile ValôÙley can be traced to the earôÙliôÙest years of the EgyptôÙian kingôÙdom.ã
Here in the twenôÙty-first cenôÙtuôÙry, most of us regard honôÙey as nothôÙing more than a relôÙaôÙtiveôÙly healthy sweetôÙenôÙer. In ancient Egypt, too, it was used to improve the taste of their bread and beer, but it was also put to imporôÙtant medôÙical uses. ãBecause itãs so thick, rejects any kind of growth and conôÙtains hydroôÙgen perôÙoxôÙide, it creôÙates the perôÙfect barôÙriôÙer against infecôÙtion for wounds,ã writes SmithôÙsonôÙianãs Natasha GeilôÙing. ãThe ancient EgypôÙtians used medôÙiôÙcôÙiôÙnal honôÙey regôÙuôÙlarôÙly, makôÙing ointôÙments to treat skin and eye disôÙeases.ã They may not have been the first to do so, givôÙen that the earôÙliôÙest known uses of honôÙey are recordôÙed on SumerôÙian clay tablets, but they took respect for the stuff to a whole new levôÙel, describôÙing honôÙeyôÙbees as origôÙiôÙnatôÙing from the tears of their sun god Re (forôÙmerôÙly known in the EngôÙlish-speakôÙing world as Ra).
That parôÙticôÙuôÙlar piece of mytholôÙoôÙgy is recordôÙed on some EgyptôÙian papyri; othôÙers reveal how much honôÙey was rationed to workôÙers, at least those employed directôÙly by the Pharaoh. In those days, the subôÙstanceãs goldôÙen colôÙor reflectôÙed its dearôÙness, and it seems that comôÙmon laborôÙers and their famôÙiôÙlies could go a lifeôÙtime withôÙout ever tastôÙing a spoonôÙful themôÙselves. Today, of course, we take it for grantôÙed that we can go down to the superôÙmarôÙket and cheapôÙly buy an econôÙoôÙmy-size tub of honôÙey that nevôÙer goes bad. But then, ancient EgyptôÙian honôÙey has nevôÙer gone bad either: thanks to the very same chemôÙiôÙcal and bioôÙlogôÙiôÙcal propôÙerôÙties that made it useôÙful for healôÙing,ô the sealed jars of it remain theôÙoôÙretôÙiôÙcalôÙly ediôÙble even after 3,000 years. DrizôÙzle it on some genôÙuineô Greek yogurt, and youãve got a large swath of the hisôÙtoôÙry of civôÙiôÙlizaôÙtion in breakôÙfast form.
via Boing Boing/SmithôÙsonôÙian
RelatôÙed conôÙtent:

A 3,000-Year-Old Painterãs Palette from Ancient Egypt, with Traces of the OrigôÙiôÙnal ColôÙors Still In It

Based in Seoul,ô ColôÙinô Marshallô writes and broadôÙcasts on cities, lanôÙguage, and culôÙture. Heãs the author of the newsletôÙterô Books on Citiesô as well as the booksô ÚõçÙ šš§ õ¡šÏô (No SumôÙmaôÙrizôÙing Korea) andô KoreôÙan Newtro.ô FolôÙlow him on the social netôÙwork forôÙmerôÙly known as TwitôÙter atô @colinmarshall.

title: When Francis Bacon Shocked the Art World: Viewers Were Horrified by His Paintings, But Couldnãt Look Away
description:
in Art, History |   	May 5th, 2026  Leave a Comment
	
			

	

A difôÙfiôÙcult childôÙhood and adoôÙlesôÙcence, satôÙuôÙratôÙed with the feelôÙing of being an
body:
in Art, History |   	May 5th, 2026  Leave a Comment
	
			

	

A difôÙfiôÙcult childôÙhood and adoôÙlesôÙcence, satôÙuôÙratôÙed with the feelôÙing of being an outôÙsider, may or may not conôÙtribute to becomôÙing a great artist. ExpeôÙriôÙencôÙing the social and culôÙturôÙal ferôÙment of Berlin and Paris in the nineôÙteen-twenôÙties probôÙaôÙbly wouldôÙnãt hurt oneãs chances. Nor, sureôÙly, would forôÙmaôÙtive expoôÙsure in such cities to films like MetropôÙoôÙlis, BatôÙtleôÙship Potemkin, and Abel Ganceãsô Napoleon, as well as to the paintôÙings of Pablo PicasôÙso. Going to art school may seem like the natôÙurôÙal choice for any aspirôÙing artist, but thereãs also someôÙthing to be gained from avoidôÙing that acaôÙdôÙeôÙmôÙic sysôÙtem entireôÙly.
These, as galôÙlerist-YoutuôÙber James Payne tells us in the new Great Art Explained video above, are all aspects of the life that proôÙduced FranôÙcis Bacon. As usuôÙal on that series, he proôÙceeds from a sinôÙgle repôÙreôÙsenôÙtaôÙtive work, in this case Study after VelûÀzquezãs PorôÙtrait of Pope InnoôÙcent X, from 1953.
If youãve seen that paintôÙing even once, you havenãt forôÙgotôÙten it, and indeed, youãve probôÙaôÙbly seen it again in your nightôÙmares since. To trace the source of its trouôÙbling powôÙer, Payne plunges into the hisôÙtoôÙry of Baconãs harôÙrowôÙing life as well as that of the Irish, EngôÙlish, and EuroôÙpean hisôÙtorôÙiôÙcal conôÙtexts in which he lived ã often to its danôÙgerôÙous, chaotôÙic fullest.
Not that any art hisôÙtoôÙriôÙan can ignore the inspiôÙraôÙtion citôÙed right there in the paintôÙingãs title. It is to that sevôÙenôÙteenth-cenôÙtuôÙry Spaniardãs acclaimed porôÙtrait of that head of the Catholic Church (who proôÙnounced the finôÙished work ãtropôÙpo veroã) that Bacon pays twistôÙed, deconôÙstrucôÙtive homage. Yet despite havôÙing been to Rome, he nevôÙer actuôÙalôÙly saw the origôÙiôÙnal; that, as Payne explains, ãwould have meant facôÙing its powôÙer directôÙly.ã Instead, he worked from a small, washed-out ãcopy of a copy,ã all the betôÙter to allow for not just reinôÙvenôÙtion, but also the incorôÙpoôÙraôÙtion of othôÙer scraps of the rapidôÙly expandôÙing mass media of the twenôÙtiôÙeth cenôÙtuôÙry: the periôÙod, despite the out-of-time qualôÙiôÙty of so much of his art, to which Bacon so thorôÙoughôÙly belonged.

title: Gijs Van Vaerenbergh Gracefully Reimagines a 16th-Century Belgian Abbey Church in Steel
description: In the late 12th century, a nobleman named Count Gerard van Loon commissioned an abbey to serve as his final resting place. Over the next few deca
body:
In the late 12th century, a nobleman named Count Gerard van Loon commissioned an abbey to serve as his final resting place. Over the next few decades, amid plenty of political tumult, Herkenrode Abbey in Hasselt, Belgium, was converted to the first Cistercian convent for women. It was a site of pilgrimage from the 13th to the 15th centuries, and despite regional wars and economic uncertainty, it stayed the course. During the 16th century, it experienced its heyday thanks to the patronage of a figure named Prince Bishop Evrard van der Marck, seeing the addition of a Gothic church that brimmed with beautiful stained glass windows, textiles, paintings, and more.
The Eighty Yearsã War paused Herkenrodeãs prosperity, and once things stabilized again politically, the abbey experienced several decades of good fortune, although much of this wealth was spent on the abbessesã own acquisitions of property and art in a show of their prestige. But the paradigm-shifting Liû´ge Revolution, which coincided with the French Revolution, brought all of this crashing to a halt by 1796. The abbey complex was promptly sold and dismantled.

Throughout the 19th and 20th centuries, Heckenrode went through many other uses, including factories and even a series of private homes, before it was again acquired by a religious organization in the 1970s. While the original 16th-century abbey church no longer exists due to a devastating fire in 1826, the site remains one of the regionãs most culturally significant. And Herita has been working to restore it. As part of a phased regeneration of the landmark and its park, an ethereal, life-size sculpture of the abbey titled CLAUSURA by Gijs Van Vaerenbergh has risen from the buildingãs original footprint.
The studio, founded by Belgian designers Pieterjan Gijs and Arnout Van Vaerenbergh, conceived of CLAUSURA as ãan artistic vision for the vanished heart of Herkenrode.ã True to scale, the structure is made of slender steel rods that rise from the ground in an airy framework. The installation revolves around the idea of memory and sensation: rather than rebuilding the abbey to try to mirror what it may have looked like hundreds of years ago, the work nods to its past with an airy elegance. 
ãThe new volumes are transparent, allowing their silhouettes to blend seamlessly with the landscape in the background,ã says a statement. ãThe intervention balances between reconstruction and abstraction, as the original structures are evoked through a refined play of suggestion. Iconic details such as windows, vaults, and towers enhance the sense of recognisability, although at times, these elements dissolve back into a chaos of lines.ã

Gijs Van Vaerenbergh is known for its architectural interventions, often utilizing steel, stone, wood, and a wide range of other building materials to re-envision spaces as structural sculptures. ãWhat unites their diverse output is a sustained focus onô how space is experiencedãvisually, bodily and temporally,ã a statement says.
CLAUSURA is being constructed in three phases. The first, which is also the most ambitious, is slated to open to the public on June 18. Visitors will be able to walk and reflect amid the installation. See more on Gijs Van Vaerenberghãs Instagram, and learn about the restoration progress and how to visit on Heritaãs website.

title: Vibrant Victorian-Era Transparencies Illuminate a Host of Microscopic Creatures
description: During the Victorian era, innovators made huge leaps with optical technologies. It was the period of the stereoscope and an early projector known
body:
During the Victorian era, innovators made huge leaps with optical technologies. It was the period of the stereoscope and an early projector known as the magic lantern, not to mention one in which eyeglasses became more affordable and entering the mainstream. These advances also influenced scientific inquiry, making microscopes more powerful, and the pursuit of microscopy enabled researchers and enthusiasts to discover creatures invisible to the naked eye.
One of these enthusiasts was London-based educator and amateur scientist Charles Thomas Hudson. Along with other scholars and aficionados, he participated in interest groups. ãAs President of the Royal Microscopical Society and a Fellow of the Royal Society, Hudson was a leading figure in this growing scientific community,ã says a statement from Osh Gallery, which is currently exhibiting a collection of unique illustrations in The Hudson Transparencies.
Sea slugs
Curated byô Luke Powell and Jody Hudson-Powell of design firm Pentagram, which runs Osh Gallery, the exhibition literally brings to light a number of colorful transparencies that Hudson used during his lectures. While they appear dark and even unfinished in a typical setting, ãwhen lit from behind these intricate works transform into magical visions of life previously only glimpsed when viewed under a microscope,ã the gallery says.
The Hudson Transparencies includes 58 original transparencies that measure a surprisingly large 37.8 by 29.5 inches. Each of the graphicsã proportions are ãthe equivalent of drawing ants the size of elephants,ã says a statement. The animals and botanicals emerge through a combination of painted paper and perforations, which are made with lines and clusters of pinholes.
Hudson was particularly fascinated by rotifers, a phylum of zooplankton named for their so-called ãwheel-bearingã characteristics. He also catalogued algae, protozoa, and larvaeãsuch as that of the mayfly with its feather-like tail. Microscopic marine organisms were a particular favorite, though, and these back-lit images highlight the convergence of science and spectacular visuals that not only brought these creatures to life in a unique way for 19th-century viewers but continue to awe us today.
The Hudson Transparencies continues through June 11 in London. You might also enjoy Martin Kunzãs turn-of-the-century tactile graphics and Lorenz Okenãs seminal natural history work,ô Allgemaine Naturgeschichte Fû¥r Alle StûÊnde.
Opercularia nutans
A larva of a mayfly
A collection of rotifer species
Testudinella patina
Sponges, Porifera
Proales werneckii
Cupelopagis vorax

title: We Spent a Week Quarantined on an Uninhabited Island with 80 Artists
description: A muscular Englishman in a khaki kilt and black beret hops atop the edge of an old well clad in traditional Spanish tile, his sleeves rolled up to
body:
A muscular Englishman in a khaki kilt and black beret hops atop the edge of an old well clad in traditional Spanish tile, his sleeves rolled up to his elbows in what can only be called an act of bravery.ô  High winds and rain pelt a group of visitors from all directions, and yet, this charismatic performer stands tall above the cobblestone to announce that heãs been living on this vacant island for nearly two centuries. Heãs here to give us a tour.
ãThis has been my home for 174 years,ã the man says, introducing himself as Captain Horatio Hollingwood. ãI arrived in command of a well-known British merchant ship, responsible for transporting goods of every sort. But alongside grain, wool, and oil, there travelled with us certain rather unwelcome companionsãterrible diseases. We stopped here for a sanitary inspection. And from here, I never left.ã
Photo by Christopher Jobson
As our group endures the Balearic Islandsã mercurial spring weather and shivers among towering stone walls and outbuildings, this exuberant actor introduces us to the Lazaretto of Mahû°n, an 18th-century fortress and infirmary that once housed merchants, shipping crews, and any travelers seeking entry to Spain. His ability to rouse a group of studio artists into the turbulent outdoors is a fitting introduction to the activities of the week ahead. Alongside nearly 80 others from Slovakia to Argentina, Washington D.C. to Melbourne, weãre here on this small, uninhabited island for Quarantine, a residency-style program conceived by artist Carles Gomila, who is determined to help artists break free from creative blocks while giving them permission to fail, discover, iterate, and hopefully, discover something new about themselves.ô 
For seven days, participants follow a rigorous schedule, arriving by boat on the island by 8:30 a.m. and leaving no earlier than 9:30 p.m. Their days are filled with talks, workshops, and meetings with invited artists who serve as mentors, the schedule of which isnãt shared in advance. Phones, laptops, and any device with an internet connection are banned, and thereãs no option to retreat to a hotel bed or wander off for an afternoon. Such a demanding and purposefully opaque schedule invites artists to settle into discomfort, abandon expectations, and confront the insecurities and anxieties capable of stifling their best work. The theme of this edition is Tears in Rain, which takes its name from the iconic monologue at the end of Ridley Scottãs Blade Runner.
ãWhat I wish now is to share with you the story of the people who lived here over the past centuries,ã the actor continues. ãYour quarantine, unlike theirs, is not compulsory. You have chosen to be here, to experience something meaningful in the way you live your lives and understand your creative process. This is a space and a time for transformation. Some passed here from life into death. Yours is a passage from blockage into freedom. Follow me.ã
Getting to the island is no small featãour journey from Chicago took two days and required three flights, a car trip, a 15-minute walk, and a short boat rideãand thereãs no open, public access to the lazaretto. Given its remote location and secret programming, Quarantine asks interested artists to apply on a true leap of faith, one that many describe as the first moment they had to relinquish control and believe the bold claims the program boasts. Testimonials include lofty statements about the organizers ãminting a legion for the revolutionã and how participants feel ãlike my insides have been blown out.ã Some people even get the programãs tally logo tattooed, and many have returned for multiple visits.
Photo by Christopher Jobson
If youãre thinking this sounds like a cult, youãre not alone. When Quarantineãs organizers invited us to observe the April 2026 edition, we were skeptical, and so were the friends and colleagues with whom we shared our plans. As it turns out, many of the participants had similar reservations, which we learned when we landed in Menorca and met an artist at baggage claim. (In keeping with the spirit of Quarantine and the idea that what happens on the island stays on the island, weãre only sharing information about participants anonymously.) He was coming from Los Angeles and first encountered the program through one of the sessionãs mentors, Yuko Shimizu, whom heãd long admired and previously collaborated with. Lured by the opportunity to untether from daily life and connect with professional artists, he hoped to reinvigorate his practice and figure out how to take the next step, something former Quarantine participants lauded and that he hoped he could access, too. Was it a cult, though? None of us was sure.
From the 18th to 20th century, the Spanish government required all travelers, no matter their origin, to sequester on the island for 40 days or if they fell ill, longer. These groups were cordoned off by their presumed and actual illnesses, and about five percent died during their stay, succumbing to infectious diseases like the Bubonic Plague and Yellow Fever. Today, the double-walled sanatorium is mid-restoration as the local government repairs crumbling limestone halls and terracotta walkways and trims back an abundance of thistles. Along with a handful of loquacious peacocks whose eerie calls echoed across the island, just a skeleton grounds crew and the occasional tour group occupy the island with any regularity.
Quarantine is one of two recurring events held on the lazaretto, with weeklong editions each April and October that are supported by the local government and local tourism organization, Fundaciû° Foment del Turisme de Menorca. Nearly everything needed for the program must be loaded onto boats and carried to the island for every edition, and a local caterer packs food for 80 and traverses the harbor each lunch and dinner. Enormous musical instruments like the bilasãa rare, standing contraption of flat bells conceived by Russian Alexander Zhikharevãeven make their way over for live, outdoor performances.
A sort of mystical bootcamp for artists, Quarantine is both intensely communal and unabashedly introspective. Gomila designs the workshop sessions, known as the ãArt Lab,ã to tap into as many emotions and responses as possible, often frustration, confusion, and eventually, clarity. Many incorporate music, and almost all center on life drawing, whether through self-portraiture or enthusiastic models who embrace the spirit of the project as much as the participants. They don costumes, hold sabres as props, and accessorize to an outlandish extent. Models are invited to share in the creative process, too, and as one tells us one evening over glasses of Cava, the program allows her to reconnect with the self she doesnãt always encounter in her life as an architect.
Photo by Christopher Jobson
Everyone we meet at Quarantine echoes this sentiment, whether theyãre full-time artists or not. Thereãs a young father whose work at a video game design studio is forcing him to rely more and more on A.I. A fine art educator laments the corporatization of her position as a faculty member at a for-profit university. And countless others who work in tech, finance, government, design, and illustration have ventured to the Mediterranean to reclaim focus, hone their voice, and if theyãre lucky, make something that excites them.
The accomplished group of mentors doesnãt hurt either. Aprilãs edition included Shimizu, Martin Wittfooth, Mu Pan, Phil Hale, Yulia Bas, Sean Layh, and Adam Miller, while past sessions featured Miles Johnston, Jeremy Mann, and NicolûÀs Uribe, to name a few. Mentors each present a morning masterclass on a wide range of topics, from Wittfoothãs concept of art as a ãspirit artifactã to Shimizuãs courage in changing careers after a decade in a corporate job. Layh shares his story of picking up his paintbrush for the first time in more than a decade to re-learn his abilities over two and a half years on a single canvas (last month he won an Archibald Prize). Participants also receive one-on-one sessions with three mentors, in which no topics are off limits. They can ask for guidance in developing a particular technique, although most choose to utilize their 45-minute sessions to chat about more personal problems theyãve both faced and connect about what it means to be an artist in todayãs world.ô 
This equalizing ethos is the foundation of Quarantine. When participants complete an exercise, all work is displayed on a central table, and if theyãd like, they can share something with the group. Thereãs no critique, no comparison, and no need to explain why they made the decisions they did. The focus instead is on the process, on seizing moments of low-risk spontaneity. Experimentation and abandoning patterns that no longer serve their creativity are encouraged, along with developing practices to work through frustrations and insecurities. The wide range of skills is liberating: many artists have worked full-time for more than a decade, while others are painting with oils for the very first time.
ãWhat happens here is so psychological,ã shares one participant from Argentina who heard about Quarantine by following Layh. ãBecause itãs all so mysterious, I was worried it was going to be cheesy, but Iãve cried three times this week.ã
Photo by Romas Tauras
On the final day, after participants have painted and sketched for dozens of hours, been subjected to creative exercises they hope to never encounter again and others they will gladly replicate at home, and let themselves be vulnerable in a way that rarely happens outside a therapistãs office, what seems to stand out is the camaraderie and an overwhelming sense of belonging. In comparison to the eager anxieties of the first day, the group has settled into a shared clarity, knowing not to fear mistakes and feeling a new sense of kinship among like-minded peers. They pair off to get coffee, encourage one another to try a strange technique, and make plans to meet up once they return home. We were told that WhatsApp group chats from previous editions continue to this day. A large contingent from a previous year also wants to return en masse.ô 
The last evening under a star-studded sky, unusually visible to us city dwellers, a fire pit appeared adjacent to the well that the Englishman jumped atop on day one. All 80 of us gathered around, and one mentor, Bas, kicked us off. In her hands were an old letter that once held significant weight in her life and a work on paper. She walked over to the fire and tossed both in, then asked everyone else to do the same.ô 
As the fire pit grew so full of paintings and drawings and sketches and notes that pieces spilled onto the cobblestone, the communal sense of catharsis and release was palpable. Artists danced hand in hand, cried, hugged, and stood solemnly watching their breakthroughs crumble into ash. The idea, of course, was that these material objectsãthese ãspirit artifactsã in Wittfoothãs parlanceãwere just that: artifacts. Artworks made on the island were both irreplaceable and irrelevant, as the program had already built up a herd immunity to any sense of assuredness or control. What Quarantine offers instead is a shared pathology, one that focuses not on remedying the symptoms of creative blocks or failures but rather zeroes in on the underlying cause.
Photo by Christopher Jobson
Photo by Romas Tauras
Photo by Romas Tauras

schema.headline: We Spent a Week Quarantined on an Uninhabited Island with 80 Artists
